sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 30, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Player at row 32-35, col 32-35. Center-below frame (node 9) is now 'G'. Frames become 'G' when player is in the same column range (adjacent above or below). Right frame was 'G' when player was at col 44-47. Left frame is still 'c' at col 16-19.
- Goal model: Need to activate all 3 frames. May need to visit each one.
- Plan: Move LEFT to col 16-19 to activate the left frame.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 29 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Looking at the image, the player (green body) is now below the left blue square frame. The left frame appears to have changed to a lighter gray color (maybe 'G'). Let me check the segmentation to confirm.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("Player position:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ['W', 'N']:
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

print("\nFrames:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ['c', 'G']:
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The left frame (node 3) is still 'c', not 'G'. The player is at row 32-35, col 16-19, which is below the left frame at row 28-31, col 16-19. But the frame didn't change to 'G'.
Wait, the player's white head is at [[32, 16], [35, 16]] - that's a vertical strip at col 16, rows 32-35. The green body is at [[32, 17], [32, 19], [35, 19], [35, 17]] - that's rows 32-35, cols 17-19.
So the player is at row 32-35, col 16-19. The left frame is at row 28-31, col 16-19. The player is directly below the frame. But the frame is still 'c'.
Earlier, when the player was at row 28-31, col 44-47 (below the right frame at row 24-27), the right frame became 'G'. The difference is:
- Right frame: row 24-27, player at row 28-31 (1 row gap between frame bottom at 27 and player top at 28)
- Left frame: row 28-31, player at row 32-35 (1 row gap between frame bottom at 31 and player top at 32)
Both have a 1-row gap, so that shouldn't matter.
Wait, let me check the player's orientation. The white head is at col 16 (left edge), and the green body is at cols 17-19. This is different from the usual orientation where the white head is on top and the green body is below.
Actually, looking at the image, the player appears to be rotated or in a different orientation. The green body is to the right of the white head, not below it. This is unusual.
Hmm, let me reconsider. Maybe the player's orientation changed when moving LEFT. Or maybe the player is now in a different state.
Actually, looking more carefully at the boundary:
- White head: [[32, 16], [35, 16]] - this is a 4x1 vertical strip at col 16, rows 32-35
- Green body: [[32, 17], [32, 19], [35, 19], [35, 17]] - this is a 4x3 rectangle at rows 32-35, cols 17-19
So the player is a 4x4 block at rows 32-35, cols 16-19, with the white head on the left edge and the green body filling the rest. This is a rotated player!
Wait, but earlier the player had the white head on top (row 28) and green body below (rows 29-31). Now the white head is on the left (col 16) and green body is to the right (cols 17-19).
This suggests the player rotated when moving LEFT. Or maybe the player's orientation depends on the direction of movement.
Actually, this might explain why the left frame didn't activate. The player is at row 32-35, but the frame is at row 28-31. The player is below the frame, but maybe the frame only activates when the player is in a specific orientation or position.
Let me try moving UP to get closer to the left frame. The player is at row 32-35, frame at row 28-31. If I move UP, the player would be at row 28-31, which overlaps with the frame.
Actually wait, the player is at row 32-35 and the frame is at row 28-31. There's a 1-row gap (row 31 is the frame bottom, row 32 is the player top). If I move UP, the player would be at row 28-31, which would overlap with the frame.
Let me try moving UP to see if the frame activates when the player is inside it.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Player at row 32-35, col 16-19, below the left frame (row 28-31, col 16-19). Frame is still 'c'. Player orientation changed - white head on left, green body on right. All frames are 'c' now (right and center-below reverted when player moved away).
Goal model: Need to understand why left frame didn't activate. May need to enter the frame (overlap) rather than just be adjacent.
Plan: Move UP to enter the left frame and see if it activates.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move UP to enter the left frame
action(['UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
